title: SCOTUS Arguments in Dobbs: Three Abolitionist Takeaways and One Prediction
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman live-analyzes the Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health oral arguments, identifying three abolitionist takeaways (Mississippi declined the equal-protection argument, the pro-abortion side has no coherent answer to stare decisis or bodily-autonomy probes, and Roberts is openly maneuvering for a 15-week compromise) and predicts a 60% chance of compromise, 20% full reversal, and 20% Roe upheld. He insists that whatever the Court does, Christians retain a duty to demand immediate abolition through state defiance of Roe.
audiences: state legislator considering an abolition bill

questions.question: What are the four ways Silberman says the Supreme Court could rule in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health?
questions.answer: Silberman lists four possible outcomes: (1) strike the 15-week ban by reaffirming an absolute pre-viability right to abortion, (2) uphold the ban by declaring the Constitution neutral and returning the issue to Congress and the states, (3) uphold the ban by rolling the Casey line back from roughly 20 weeks to 15 weeks or some other arbitrary point, or (4) uphold the rights to life and equal protection by abolishing abortion outright. Only the fourth, he argues, is what the court ought to do.
questions.quote:
1. Strike down the 15-week ban by upholding the supposed absolute right to abortion pre-viability.
2. Uphold the 15-week ban by ruling that the Constitution is neutral on abortion and thereby leave the issue to Congress and the States.
3. Uphold the 15-week ban by compromising and rolling back the line established in *Planned Parenthood v. Casey* from roughly 20 weeks to 15 weeks or some other arbitrary line before 15 weeks.
4. Uphold the rights to life and equal protection by abolishing abortion.

questions.question: Why does Silberman fault Mississippi's litigation strategy even while praising Solicitor General Stewart for refusing to hedge?
questions.answer: Silberman credits Stewart for refusing the compromise route in his exchange with Thomas, but criticizes the state for advancing a 'Constitution is neutral on abortion' theory rather than the equal-protection argument that preborn children are persons. He says jettisoning the equal-protection claim is harmful long-term because, if life begins at conception, the only constitutionally faithful outcome is abolition.
questions.quote: He argued that the Constitution is neutral on the subject of abortion, jettisoning the less popular but more Biblically and Constitutionally faithful argument that preborn children are fully equal in value to everyone else and should be afforded the equal protection of the law. If life begins at conception — and everyone knows it does, despite the pro-abortion justices pretending it’s a mystery — there is no option but abolition given the Constitutional guarantees of life and equal protection. This decision by Stewart is harmful to the cause long-term.

questions.question: How does Silberman characterize the historical posture of pro-life leaders, and what does the Casey decision illustrate about it?
questions.answer: He describes the movement as 'defined by leaders who exhibit extreme timidity and tolerance for compromise,' citing Casey as the most crushing defeat in its history because an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority voted to maintain legal abortion. He points to Focus on the Family VP Gary Bauer claiming victory simply because some regulations survived as proof of that compromising posture.
questions.quote: The Pro-Life Movement has long been defined by leaders who exhibit extreme timidity and tolerance for compromise. The *Casey*decision, in which an 8-1 Republican-appointed majority voted to maintain legal abortion, was the most crushing defeat in the history of the Pro-Life Movement.

questions.question: What did Justice Alito's hypothetical about Plessy v. Ferguson expose in Solicitor General Prelogar's stare decisis defense of Roe?
questions.answer: Alito asked whether Plessy could have been overruled in 1897 simply because it was egregiously wrong on the day it was handed down, with no new facts. Prelogar refused to answer yes or no and kept retreating to other stare decisis factors, which Silberman says exposed her position as untenable: she could not say yes without conceding Roe could likewise be overruled for being wrong, nor say no without endorsing 60 years of state-mandated segregation.
questions.quote: In the more than three-minute exchange, Prelogar was twisted into a knot and refused to plainly answer Alito’s question. She couldn’t give a plain “yes” because she had already argued for keeping *Roe* and *Casey* on the grounds that new information is always necessary in order to overturn precedent. But she couldn’t say “no” because she doesn’t want to admit that the supreme court needed to wait 60 years to overturn state-mandated segregation.

questions.question: How does Silberman use Julie Rikelman's drug-use answer to attack the bodily-autonomy argument?
questions.answer: Thomas pressed Rikelman on whether a woman could be liable for child neglect for harming her preborn child via drug use; Rikelman conceded states can regulate drug use throughout pregnancy. Silberman points out the contradiction: at, say, 8 weeks the same child can be lawfully killed but cannot be non-lethally harmed, which he compares to antebellum white-supremacist legal schizophrenia about counting black persons.
questions.quote: She admits that in the exact same moment in the child’s life — say, 8 weeks — she believes that states cannot protect that child from being murdered, while affirming that states can protect the child from harm caused by drug use. In Rikelman’s world, you can’t non-lethally harm preborn children, but you can rip their heads off. This is no different than the legal schizophrenia of antebellum white supremacists who wanted black people to count as people for population counting and other purposes, but did not want black people to have the rights and protections afforded to citizens.

questions.question: Why does Silberman believe Chief Justice Roberts is positioning the Court for a 15-week compromise rather than full reversal?
questions.answer: He points to Roberts repeatedly steering Rikelman back to the 15-week line and his comments that the undue burden standard need not be tethered to viability. Silberman reads this as a clear effort to retain Casey's undue-burden framework while merely shifting the line backward from viability to about 15 weeks.
questions.quote: Roberts didn’t want to address going any further than 15-weeks, and was making the case for 15 weeks as a moderate compromise. Combine this line of questioning with Roberts’ statements throughout the hearing about the undue burden standard not necessarily being tethered to the viability line and you get a very clear picture of a man intending to maintain the same undue burden standard laid out in *Casey* while rolling back the line from viability to around 15 weeks.

questions.question: Why does Silberman doubt that Kavanaugh, Gorsuch, or Barrett will join Thomas in fully overturning Roe?
questions.answer: He notes that no other justice has ever signed onto Thomas's prior opinions calling Roe and Casey 'grievously wrong,' and that the three Trump appointees have 'been very cautiously avoiding issuing broad rulings in favor of social conservatives' so far in their SCOTUS careers. Combined with Roberts's near-certain opposition to a full reversal, that leaves only Alito as a likely Thomas ally.
questions.quote: But I saw nothing from any of the other three justices that would lead me to believe they back a full reversal in this case. As a point to the contrary, all three have been very cautiously avoiding issuing broad rulings in favor of social conservatives thus far in their SCOTUS careers.

questions.question: What probabilities does Silberman assign to each possible Dobbs outcome?
questions.answer: He estimates a 60 percent chance the Court adopts a 15-week compromise that preserves the undue-burden framework, a 20 percent chance of total reversal of Roe and Casey, and a 20 percent chance the Court doubles down and upholds Roe in full.
questions.quote: To put it in percentages, I would say there’s a 60 percent chance of a 15-week compromise, 20 percent chance of total reversal, and 20 percent chance of the court doubling down (for the umpteenth time) on *Roe*.

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue a 15-week compromise would actually be the worst possible outcome?
questions.audience: state legislator considering an abolition bill
questions.answer: He warns that a compromise ruling would let pro-lifers and pro-choicers alike treat the case as the demise of Roe while in fact preserving the undue-burden framework, merely shifting the protected line by five weeks. He links to Free the States' rapid-reaction video arguing this would be 'the worst case scenario for preborn children and the cause of justice.'
questions.quote: The problem with this thinking is that even if it is correct, there is no guarantee the court will overturn its abortion precedents completely rather than simply roll back the line five weeks; which, for reasons explained in our [rapid reaction video earlier this afternoon](https://www.facebook.com/FreeTheStates/videos/669179817800368), would actually be the worst case scenario for preborn children and the cause of justice.

questions.question: What duty does Silberman say Christians and Americans retain regardless of how Dobbs is decided?
questions.answer: He argues the Court's opinion does not alter every American's immutable obligation to demand officials uphold the Constitution and Christ's law by abolishing abortion, framing it as a duty to 'rescue those being led to slaughter.' Calling for state defiance of Roe is, he says, the only Biblically justifiable response.
questions.quote: Of course, none of these possibilities effect the immutable obligations of every American. Whatever the court has said in past opinions or will opine in this case, you, brother or sister, have an obligation to demand your governing officials obey Christ and uphold the Constitution by abolishing abortion. You have a duty to rescue those being led to slaughter.

questions.question: Why does Silberman argue that calling for nullification is actually the most pragmatic path to overturning Roe?
questions.audience: state legislator considering an abolition bill
questions.answer: He claims that calling for abolition through ignoring Roe is the best way to get the Court itself to overturn Roe, because if the justices sense the states and the people are about to abolish abortion and expose the Court as a paper tiger, they will be more likely to correct their wrongs.
questions.quote: Further, calling for abolition through [ignoring *Roe*](https://freethestates.org/nullification-and-interposition/index.html) is the best way to get the court to overturn *Roe*. If they sense the states and the people are on the verge of abolishing abortion and exposing the court as a paper tiger, the justices will be more likely to correct their wrongs.

questions.question: What 'wildcard' scenario does Silberman warn abolitionists not to overlook?
questions.answer: He raises the possibility of Republican-appointed justices defecting to uphold Casey and Roe in full, citing the historical precedent of Souter, Kennedy, and O'Connor banding together in Casey despite being Reagan and Bush appointees. If an 8-1 Republican majority could uphold legal abortion then, the current 6-3 (or really 5-4 once Roberts's 'consistent betrayals' are counted) certainly could now.
questions.quote: Another outcome that can’t be overlooked is the possibility of wildcard betrayals that lead to *Casey* and *Roe* being upheld in full. In *Casey*, no one saw coming the coalition of Reagan and Bush-appointees (David Souter, Anthony Kennedy, and Sandra Day O’Connor) who joined to determine the outcome of the case in favor of legal child sacrifice.

related.title: 4 Abolitionist Rapid Reactions to the (Wrongly Decided) Dobbs Decision
related.reason: The follow-up after the Dobbs decision actually came down — directly tests Silberman's predictions about compromise vs. reversal.

related.title: Analyzing Alito's Leaked Draft, How Roe Died, and Where We Go From Here
related.reason: The leaked-draft analysis written between this oral-argument piece and the final ruling, building on the same Alito-centric reading.

related.title: Abolitionist Lawyer to File Amicus Brief in SCOTUS Abortion Case
related.reason: Background on the equal-protection amicus brief filed in Dobbs that Silberman implicitly contrasts with Mississippi's neutrality argument.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Sets up Roberts's June Medical betrayal that this article cites as the basis for predicting his Dobbs vote.

related.title: Supreme Court Once Again Rejects Pro-Life Regulation
related.reason: Free the States' contemporaneous account of the June Medical loss that anchors Silberman's read of Roberts.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Companion theoretical case for why a 15-week compromise — the predicted Dobbs outcome — would be a defeat, not a victory.

related.title: Equal Protection
related.reason: Lays out the equal-protection / personhood argument Silberman faults Mississippi for abandoning at oral argument.
